claim: Short-term memory in agents maps to recent conversational history and working memory; long-term memory includes semantic, episodic, and procedural memory types
actionable: Structure agent memory as layers: short-term (context window), semantic (external knowledge bases), episodic (past interactions/events), and procedural (learned skills/system prompts)
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Semantic memory in agents comes from LLM weights plus external knowledge bases; episodic memory is sequences of past actions/tasks; procedural memory is agent code and system prompts
actionable: Model each memory type separately: feed semantic knowledge via RAG tools, log episodic memories to database (task descriptions + steps), update procedural memory via dynamic system prompts
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Memory creation should extract specific insights (character traits, relationships, error resolutions) from reasoning traces and tool outputs rather than storing raw details
actionable: Prompt agents to summarize and extract actionable insights from their interactions when creating memories, filtering out noise to keep long-term storage efficient
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Trigger memory creation when context window approaches capacity, on fixed intervals (every N turns), or at conversation end to prevent redundant storage and manage latency
actionable: Set memory creation cadence based on your use case: high-stakes tasks → every action, chat → every N turns or when context ~80% full, conversation-end → final summary
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Retrieve long-term memory using exact matching (character/entity names), vector search (conceptual similarity), hybrid search (keyword + semantic), and rescoring by recency/importance
actionable: Implement multi-tier memory retrieval: exact match for known entities, vector search for conceptual queries, then rescore with timestamp decay and importance scores
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: All memories should include timestamps (created/updated) to enable filtering by recency, phasing out old memories, and applying decay functions during retrieval
actionable: Add created_at and updated_at timestamps to every memory record; apply exponential decay to retrieval scores favoring recent memories for better conversational continuity
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Update memories by retrieving relevant old memories, reconciling with new information, and writing back to external storage in a single CRUD operation to maintain consistency
actionable: When agents learn new information, implement: retrieve old memory → merge with new info → reconcile conflicts → write updated memory back to database to prevent hallucinations
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Delete old unused memories via lifecycle policies (archive cold data, delete after retention period) to control storage costs and improve retrieval latency as memory grows
actionable: Monitor memory usage patterns; move memories unused for 6+ months to cheaper storage; delete after 1-2 years unless flagged as important to keep search space manageable
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Agents can update their system prompts dynamically by creating memory of user instructions then retrieving and incorporating them, enabling behavioral adaptation without code changes
actionable: When users give agents instructions (e.g., 'always include docstrings'), store as procedural memory and have the agent retrieve and apply them in system prompt updates
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents

claim: Memory inconsistencies and poor reconciliation cause agent hallucination; agents must update long-term memory accurately to maintain reliability and reduce false claims
actionable: Implement memory validation: when an agent retrieves conflicting memories, prompt it to reconcile before responding; test memory accuracy regularly to catch hallucinations early
source_title: AI Dev 25 | Apoorva Joshi: Building Agents That Learn—Managing Memory in AI Agents
